text: Four robotic ports are positioned horizontally, two centimeters above the umbilicus, with an auxiliary port on the left side. Pneumoperitoneum is established using the air seal system.

The surgeon opens the lesser omentum and places a tissue tape around the hepatic pedicle for vascular control. A plastic tube is introduced into the abdomen, and a clamping test is performed. Intraoperative ultrasonography is used to assess future resection margins. The triangular ligament is opened, and the left liver lobe is progressively mobilized.

The surgeon performs proximal dissection at the falciform ligament on the superior medial part of the left liver lobe using robotic arm number four to retract the lobe medially. Progressive dissection continues until the Arantius ligament is visualized. A hook is used to mark the parenchymal transection line on the anterior-posterior part of the left liver lobe.

The surgeon performs intermittent pedicular clamping for ten minutes while transecting the liver parenchyma using cheliclasia. The assistant irrigates the surgical field with a Maryland bipolar forceps. Vitalic clips are applied to close small venous and portal branches leading to liver segment II. The parenchymal transection continues with cheliclasia under pedicular clamping.

A haemlock clip is placed on a portal pedicle, followed by division using laparoscopic scissors. The specimen is progressively mobilized, with continued transection in the proximal part. Dissection with cheliclasia is used to selectively dissect portal venous branches. The specimen is retracted to the left, then cranially and medially, to facilitate further transection.

The surgeon divides the last remaining branches while the assistant holds the suction system next to the bipolar forceps to prevent smoke-induced fogging. The transection line is coagulated using a hook, and the final parenchymal attachments are separated.

A Hem-o-lok clip is placed on the tissue, followed by an additional clip. Lateral traction is applied to the specimen, and a final Hem-o-lok clip is secured. Scissors are then used to divide the tissue.

The specimen is placed into an ender bag. The transection line is coagulated using monopolar cautery, and the tape used for vascular control is withdrawn. The surgical field is inspected, confirming no drain placement at the end of the procedure.
